findings.message: The Phase 2 classification for `S ∩ C` conflicts with the skip rule below it: the table marks structural matches as `🔵`, but the later `skip` rule says `action=Reference` + structural equality should be suppressed instead of reported. As written, the same case is both a blue signal and a skip.

`D3` says this is a `10 領域` table, but the table itself enumerates 11 distinct conditions, and the consequence note repeats the same `10 領域` claim. The count is internally inconsistent; either the heading/bullets need to say 11 or one row needs to be merged.

`transition_methods` is allowed to reference `trait_impls`内の `MethodDeclaration` here, but D10 later removes `methods` from `TraitImplDecl` entirely. That leaves no schema location for the referenced method declarations, so the typestate transition path is internally inconsistent as written.

The Phase 1 `Delete` step removes an item from `S` but never rewrites or rejects surviving references to that item. For any deletion of a still-referenced baseline item, `S` will end up with dangling `Id`s, so the stated fully-resolved `S` cannot actually be constructed.

ADR 1 says the signal evaluator refines action semantics into a "10-region" table, but the referenced ADR 3 D3 defines an 11-region table and repeats that count later. Because ADR 1 explicitly points readers to ADR 3 D3 as the canonical region definition, this cross-reference leaves implementers with conflicting signal-table cardinality.

This assigns implicit Reference resolution for undeclared baseline-derived items to the codec, but ADR 2 D10 keeps the A codec open-world and defers resolution to S construction, while ADR 3 D4 says the signal evaluator dynamically attaches Reference to B. That is a component-boundary contradiction and can lead to implementing baseline-dependent behavior in the catalogue codec.

D is defined as the set of B-derived items declared for deletion, but the pseudocode adds the A/catalogue element to D (`D.add(elt rebuild_id)`) rather than the removed baseline item from S/B. Following this literally makes D contain the delete declaration shape instead of the baseline item, which contradicts the D definition and can corrupt delete progress evaluation and D-specific external_crates rebuilding.

The signal distribution summary omits `S \ C + action=Add`, even though the table immediately above classifies that region as Yellow. This makes the summary inconsistent with the canonical table and risks dropping the new-declare-not-yet-implemented case from implementations that follow the summary.

ADR 3 defines the Phase 2 table as 11 regions, but ADR 1 still references a '10-region' signal table. That leaves the catalogue ADR pointing at the wrong evaluator contract for action semantics; update the ADR 1 D4/Related text to match ADR 3's 11-region table, or adjust ADR 3 if 10 was intended.

The canonical schema prose introduces `ItemAction`, and ADR 2/3 consume `ItemAction`, but the ADR 1 schema snippets declare `action: TypeAction` for TypeEntry/TraitEntry/FunctionEntry. This is a semantic type-name mismatch in the plan artifacts and will send implementers toward a non-selected action type.

This states `S.external_crates` is the full union of B and A, but the same paragraph says deletes can remove dependencies from S and leave them only in D. A full B∪A union cannot drop a baseline-only dependency used solely by a deleted item, so the S/D construction contract is internally inconsistent and may make Phase 2 compare against the wrong target graph.

D is defined as the set of B-derived items being deleted, but the Delete branch iterates over A and adds `elt` to D. That contradicts the definition and can lose the baseline item's actual structure/references; the branch should make clear that D receives the matched baseline/S item removed by the delete operation, not the catalogue delete declaration itself.

ADR 2 cites its own D6 as the decision establishing '1 catalogue = 1 crate', but D6 is about fixed values for rustdoc fields; the crate-per-catalogue decision is ADR 1 D6. This broken decision reference appears in the codec rationale and rejected alternative text, so readers are directed to the wrong design decision.

D1 says the Layer axis is determined implicitly from catalogue file placement, but D6 defines an explicit `layer: Layer` field inside `CatalogueDocument` and says the document declares its layer. This leaves the schema contract ambiguous: implementers cannot tell whether layer is derived from path/layout or parsed from the JSON field.

D1 states that A/S extend `rustdoc_types::Crate` with action only and that role/pattern/docs remain on the Catalogue side, but D7 later says catalogue `docs` are encoded into rustdoc `Item.docs`. Those two rules conflict over whether docs are part of the TypeGraph produced by the codec.

The Related section still describes the signal evaluator ADR as a future planned ADR, while this ADR already references the concrete ADR 3 decisions earlier and the scoped change includes that ADR. This makes the cross-document relationship stale and self-contradictory; it should point to `2026-05-08-0305-tddd-signal-evaluator-three-way-diff.md` as the sibling/follow-up ADR already present.

ADR 1 D11 explicitly uses `FunctionPath` because function short names such as `new`/`build` commonly collide, but ADR 2 makes graph identity `name`-only. That means two allowed catalogue functions with the same short name in different modules collapse during matching/Id rebuild, so the plan cannot correctly evaluate a catalogue shape that ADR 1 says is valid.

The external-crate construction rule contradicts itself: this passage says `S.external_crates` is rebuilt only from items remaining after Delete, so delete-only dependencies disappear from S, but the same ADR's pseudocode and Reassess section describe `S.external_crates = B ∪ A`, and ADR 2 D5 says to union B and A external crates. Those two rules produce different S graphs whenever a deleted baseline item was the only user of an external crate.

ADR 1 says undeclared baseline-derived entries are auto-resolved as implicit References by the codec, but ADR 2/3 place that responsibility in the signal evaluator: A codec is open-world and independent of B, while Phase 1 builds S from B with dynamic `Reference` attachment. Leaving this as-is gives conflicting ownership for whether the codec may read/resolve baseline items.

ADR 2 now defines identity as short name for types/traits but full FunctionPath for functions, while ADR 3 Phase 1 still says all identity matching and Id rebuilds are by name. That makes functions like different `new`/`build` entries collide or be matched incorrectly in S/D/C construction, contradicting ADR 1 D11 and ADR 2 D3.

The signal table classifies `S \ C + action=Add` as Yellow, but the immediately following Yellow distribution omits that region. If implementers use the distribution summary, declared-but-unimplemented adds can be dropped from Yellow reporting despite being part of the canonical table.

D1/D7 state that catalogue docs are encoded into `rustdoc Item.docs`, but D2 says `role / pattern / docs` remain only on `CatalogueDocument`. This leaves the TypeGraph/codec contract ambiguous about whether docs are present in A/S at all, despite render/documentation being listed as a consumer.

D11 says unprefixed TypeRef identifiers always mean same-catalogue declarations, but the ADR examples rely on unprefixed standard/prelude types such as `Result`, `Option`, `Vec`, and `String`. With the stated open-world/closed-world flow, those become unresolved markers and then Phase 1 errors unless the catalogue/baseline declares them, so common declared TypeRefs are physically unexecutable without an additional std/prelude resolution rule or prefix requirement.

`bool` is listed with `Vec`/`Option`/`String` as a prefix-less std item that the codec should resolve as a `"std"` external-crate reference. That contradicts the `rustdoc_types` mapping described in D9, where Rust primitives should become `Type::Primitive`; encoding `bool` as a resolved external path will make catalogue-derived A structurally differ from rustdoc-derived B/C for valid fields such as `active: bool`. Split primitive handling from std-prelude path handling.

D2 states immediately above that functions are identified by `FunctionPath`, but the action branches still do `assert elt in S (by name)` / `get_by_name`. If implemented literally, duplicate function names in different modules, which ADR 1 D11 explicitly supports via `FunctionPath`, become ambiguous during Add/Modify/Reference/Delete processing. The pseudocode needs to use the same identity key as the surrounding text: short name for types/traits, full FunctionPath for functions.

ADR 3 narrows function identity to `FunctionPath (module_path + name)`, but ADR 2 D3 defines `FunctionPath = crate_name + module_path + name` and says all segments are used. If the signal evaluator follows ADR 3, it can build identity keys that do not match the catalogue key shape from ADR 1/2, and same-module/name functions from different crates would collapse whenever graphs are aggregated. Keep the crate_name segment in the ADR 3 identity rule or explicitly state why this evaluator is strictly single-crate and how the full catalogue key is normalized.

The Delete branch rejects as soon as the deleted item is still referenced by any current S item, but the same Phase 1 loop also allows Modify to replace items. A valid change that deletes `OldType` and modifies `User` to remove its `OldType` field can be rejected if Delete is processed before Modify, making the algorithm order-dependent and contradicting the stated goal that S is the final result of applying A to B. The plan needs to apply Add/Modify replacements before dangling-reference validation, or run that validation after final S construction.

D11 now says prefixless prelude types like `Vec`, `Option`, `Result`, `String`, and `Box` are resolved as a `std` external crate, but D5 still defines `external_crates` extraction as only `TraitImplDecl.origin_crate` plus crate-prefixed `TypeRef`s. A catalogue using `Vec<UserId>` would therefore create a `ResolvedPath` to `std` without the corresponding `external_crates` entry/crate_id, making the codec rules internally inconsistent.

Phase 1 checks for dangling IDs before resolving A's unresolved TypeRef markers, then resolves those markers against raw B. If a catalogue Add/Modify references a baseline item that is also Delete-declared, the pre-resolution dangling check cannot see that reference, and `B.find_by_name` can still resolve it after the item has been moved to D. That either leaves S referencing a deleted item or reintroduces it, contradicting the stated requirement that references to deleted items be rejected as a Phase 1 Error. The marker resolution needs to run before the dangling-reference validation, or resolve only against the post-delete S universe.

ADR 3 says Delete処理後の S is the closed-world universe used to reject references to deleted types, but the same paragraph also resolves against `A.index`. Since A still contains catalogue items including Delete declarations, a reference to a deleted item can resolve through A after it was removed from S, accepting the exact case this section says must become a Phase 1 Error. Remove the A fallback or restrict it to non-delete items already retained in S.

ADR 2 still defines unresolved marker resolution as `B (Baseline) + A` and says the universe is `B + A`, while ADR 3 now defines the closed-world universe as post-delete S. Following ADR 2 would allow deleted baseline items from B to resolve unresolved TypeRefs, contradicting ADR 3's delete-after-S invariant and defeating deleted-reference rejection. Update D10 and the matching consequence text to use the post-delete S rule.

Phase 1.5 says unresolved markers must resolve only against the post-Delete S universe so references to deleted types are rejected, but the pseudocode still falls back to `A.find_by_name(...)`. Because `A` still contains Delete declarations, this branch can resolve a marker to an item that was intentionally removed from S, contradicting the stated closed-world rule and allowing the exact deleted-type reference the plan says must fail.

This consequence still says Phase 1 rejects markers that cannot resolve in `B + A`, while D10 now defines the resolver as post-Delete S only. That stale universe definition conflicts with the updated Delete handling and would preserve deleted declarations as valid resolution targets if implemented from this summary.

Rejected alternative H claims ADR 1 D10 adopted optional trait impl methods, but ADR 1 D10 actually removes `TraitImplDecl.methods` entirely and the next sentence says the same. This self-contradiction leaves the rejected-alternative rationale pointing at a decision state that no longer exists.
